argument:
Requiring AI coding agents in software engineering courses prepares students for the profession they are actually entering. Industry adoption is already widespread; graduates who cannot prompt, supervise, and debug AI-generated code will be at a disadvantage against peers who can. A curriculum that ignores these tools teaches an outdated version of the job.

Far from undermining learning, structured use builds higher-order skills. When students must read, critique, and correct an agent's output, they practice code review, specification writing, and architectural judgment — the very competencies that distinguish strong engineers from those who merely type syntax. The tool handles boilerplate so coursework can target design and verification.

Requiring the tools also promotes equity. Left optional, only the already-confident or well-resourced students adopt them, widening gaps. A requirement ensures every student gains supervised, evaluated exposure with instructor guidance on responsible use, rather than encountering agents unsupervised for the first time on the job.

Crucially, "require" means require thoughtful use, not blind acceptance. Courses can mandate that students document, test, and defend AI-assisted work, making misuse visible and teaching the discipline of trusting but verifying. Optional adoption cannot enforce that rigor. Equipping every graduate to collaborate critically with the dominant tools of their field is exactly what professional education should do.
content:
Requiring AI coding agents in software engineering courses prepares students for the profession they are actually entering. Industry adoption is already widespread; graduates who cannot prompt, supervise, and debug AI-generated code will be at a disadvantage against peers who can. A curriculum that ignores these tools teaches an outdated version of the job.

Far from undermining learning, structured use builds higher-order skills. When students must read, critique, and correct an agent's output, they practice code review, specification writing, and architectural judgment — the very competencies that distinguish strong engineers from those who merely type syntax. The tool handles boilerplate so coursework can target design and verification.

Requiring the tools also promotes equity. Left optional, only the already-confident or well-resourced students adopt them, widening gaps. Optional adoption cannot enforce that rigor. Equipping every graduate to collaborate critically with the dominant tools of their field is exactly what professional education should do.

argument:
While my opponent is right that AI coding agents reflect modern industry practice, *requiring* them in software engineering courses confuses a professional tool with a pedagogical one. The core purpose of these courses is to build the mental models beneath the code: how to decompose a problem, reason about data structures, trace control flow, and debug from first principles. When novices lean on an agent that produces plausible code instantly, they routinely skip exactly the productive struggle that forms those foundations. You cannot evaluate or correct what an agent generates if you never learned to write it yourself.

A mandate also wrongly assumes uniform readiness. Students arrive with vastly different backgrounds; forcing the same tool on everyone penalizes those who most need unmediated practice, while doing little for those already fluent. Industry adoption argues for *exposure*, not *compulsion* — graduates can learn agent workflows quickly once their fundamentals are sound, but cannot easily recover missing fundamentals later.

Finally, requiring agents raises real concerns about academic integrity, over-reliance, and the entrenchment of whatever biases or errors the tools carry. A far better policy lets instructors decide when AI assistance serves the learning objective, rather than mandating it across every course. Preparing students for industry means cultivating durable judgment first, not outsourcing the very skills the degree is meant to certify.

argument:
Opponents worry that mandating AI coding agents will erode students' grasp of fundamentals, leaving graduates unable to reason about code they did not write themselves. But this assumes the tool replaces understanding rather than reshaping what understanding now means. Professional software engineering has already moved decisively toward agent-assisted workflows; a curriculum that shields students from that reality graduates them into obsolescence. Requiring AI agents does not abolish fundamentals—it relocates the learning to higher-value skills: specifying intent precisely, reading and auditing generated code, catching subtle defects, and judging when an agent's output is wrong. These are demanding cognitive tasks, not shortcuts.

Crucially, "require" means structured, supervised use, not unconstrained delegation. Instructors can design assignments where students must critique, debug, and extend agent output, making comprehension the graded outcome rather than mere production. A student who cannot explain or correct what the agent wrote fails the assignment—so the requirement actually enforces deeper engagement than traditional coding alone.

Far from weakening foundations, mandatory exposure ensures every graduate, not just the self-directed few, learns to collaborate with the tools that now define the field. Optional adoption widens the gap; requirement closes it, distributing a critical professional competency equitably across the entire cohort.
content:
Opponents worry that mandating AI coding agents will erode students' grasp of fundamentals, leaving graduates unable to reason about code they did not write themselves. But this assumes the tool replaces understanding rather than reshaping what understanding now means. Professional software engineering has already moved decisively toward agent-assisted workflows; a curriculum that shields students from that reality graduates them into obsolescence. Requiring AI agents does not abolish fundamentals—it relocates the learning to higher-value skills: specifying intent precisely, reading and auditing generated code, catching subtle defects, and judging when an agent's output is wrong. These are demanding cognitive tasks, not shortcuts.

Crucially, "require" means structured, supervised use, not unconstrained delegation. Instructors can design assignments where students must critique, debug, and extend agent output, making comprehension the graded outcome rather than mere production. A student who cannot explain or correct what the agent wrote fails the assignment—so the requirement actually enforces deeper engagement than traditional coding alone.

Far from weakening foundations, mandatory exposure ensures every graduate, not just the self-directed few, learns to collaborate with the tools that now define the field. Optional adoption widens the gap; requirement closes it, distributing a critical professional competency equitably across the entire cohort.

argument:
Requiring AI coding agents conflates familiarity with competence. The Pro position assumes that mandating these tools prepares students for industry, but software engineering courses exist to build the foundational reasoning—algorithmic thinking, debugging from first principles, and understanding *why* code works—that AI agents tend to short-circuit. When a tool generates a plausible solution, novices lose the productive struggle through which expertise is actually formed. A mandate accelerates the wrong skill: prompting over problem-solving.

Industry alignment also cuts the other way. Tools and vendors change rapidly; the agent a university requires today may be obsolete or paywalled before students graduate. What endures is the ability to evaluate any tool critically—and you cannot critique what you never learned to do yourself. Voluntary, scaffolded exposure achieves readiness without forcing dependence.

There are also equity and integrity costs a requirement ignores. Mandated agents raise data-privacy questions, entrench reliance on commercial platforms, and make it far harder for instructors to assess genuine understanding. A blanket requirement removes the educator's judgment about *when* a tool helps and *when* it harms learning.

The goal is engineers who can reason independently and adopt new tools wisely. Compelling AI use undermines that goal. Universities should teach with these tools where pedagogically sound—never require them as a condition of learning.
